item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is an older article, but I'm trying to leave constructive criticism when I downvote:

Blackbox in the containment procedures. As soon as I got this far I downvoted. Containment procedures are to be reviewed regularly and be able to be followed by even the newest recruit. New guy comes in, reads this, and goes "Who do I tell if this thing activates?" No good.

Lead-lined box? There could be a good reason for this, but it isn't stated or inferred. Are we trying to keep MCD from having this information and have reason to believe it's bugged? Or is the lead lined box trying to keep the thing from getting readings? Why would we do this? It also sounds like it isn't working, as it can activate from within the box.

Impending doom is always nice, but feels a little tacked on with this one. I'd get rid of the message from O5 and replace the blackboxed year in the last note with a 21██, so the reader has to make the connections themselves, which can help with the fridge logic/sense of dread.

Not at all a bad idea, just some sticky points that make it cumbersome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why bother lead-lining the box? It's not emitting any kind of harmful radiation. Seems like a needless waste of resource.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's 2016 and I'm so glad I'm not living in San Francisco.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well no. There are artificial elements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Contains traces of elements not normally occuring on Earth? Like what? All elements occur on Earth, that makes no sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is not much to this one, but that's okay! Sometimes having a very straightforward weird object is better than trying to make it up to be something it is not! A machine that predicts earthquakes in the future is not the most creative idea, but it still is pretty cool in it's own right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image in this article was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact anqxyr or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It mainly bothers me that an addendum intended for Level 4 personnel has redactions. Why not include the numbers of the SCPs that are predicting apocalypse?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this one is interesting, especially with the final note from the O5, but I can't help noticing the page mentions that the device should be restocked with paper when necessary in the description. Isn't this something that should be detailed in the special containment procedures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just okay, in my book. Will take another look at it later, but for now, no vote.

EDIT: On second read, I like it. Here, have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's the kicker: an earthquake takes effect from anywhere for a few seconds to a minute or more, concentrated most heavily in a single epicenter and spreading out radially from there.

Nuclear detonations occur over a *very* short period of time, shorter than most earthquakes of a type that would be recorded by this machine, and it's the fact that a great number of high-intensity-short-duration instances occur within a tiny fraction of time across most of the heavily populated portions of the planet (and not a whole lot of other places) that makes for some damn worrisome data.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm kind of a stickler for science. As long as the effect can be explained by the fact that something is an SCP, I'm fine. But stuff like this, where there is no way that the SCP itself could explain this effect, you've got me stuck. That's just me though. A +16 rating isn't exactly bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. It's subtle, but it catches your attention.

As for the science, I'm not that concerned. That's just me, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is more of a case of the image not perfectly matching the item, and was one of my biggest fears whilst writing this. I went in and added a sentence to clear it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but I have one issue that prevents me from upvoting.

I admit that I'm no seismologist, however the fact that there are helicorders, each orientated so as to record transverse motion in a direction perpendicular to the other two makes it quite clear that the seismometer simply records motion in the X, Y and Z directions. Not amplitude, location and time.

I accept that this could be a quirk of the item, however, as the article makes no reference to this, it just looks as though the author doesn't know what they are talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Although:

we are to be weary of outside factions

I'm sure the Overseer Council is kind of exasperated with all this, but you'd expect them to have more patience. Heh.

Sorry, had to say that. I think it's interesting enough to be more than just an anomalous item. It predicts stuff from the future—it is a resource that the Foundation might want to make active use of, not just toss into a storage shed and forget about. I like it, anyhow.

Actually, to tell you the truth, the last bit sort of takes away from the article. I sort of liked it as just an ordinary thing that says stuff's gonna happen real soon. The message sounded just a bit…contrived. I don't know, I'm not the best judge on this, but if I were you I'd just omit it. Might want to get other opinions on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But what kind of earthquake measures nearly the same magnitude at multiple places across a continent? I'm sorry if it's some type of science error that's getting you, but I'm not a geology major. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But aren't magnitudes of earthquakes generally decided by multiple seismographs located at different places? Or are we just assuming that 1023 measures the epicenter. Because if so, there wouldn't really be a away to distinguish between a nuke and an earthquake…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The machine itself doesn't know they're nuclear weapons. But it lists off several large population centers, as well as Foundation Sites as the locations of seismic events, and the magnitude of these events matches with known seismic signatures created when a warhead detonates (specifically the signatures of buried on-site warheads). It's more a game of drawing conclusions than flat-out prediction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, this doesn't seem to stand alone as an SCP. It seems more like an anomalous item, especially since the kick doesn't even have to do specifically with this one. Also, how would it know about nukes if it's a seismograph? Wouldn't it just appear to be an earthquake?
